role_hints.note: Licensed hands-on care whose time IS the billing unit (timed CPT codes) — replacing therapist minutes destroys the revenue those minutes generate. The AI-touchable slice inside the role is documentation (AI scribes reclaim unbilled note-writing time, which converts to throughput, not headcount cuts).

role_hints.oews_basis: 29-1127 Speech-Language Pathologists 13.6% + 29-1122 Occupational Therapists 11.7%; audiologists (29-1181) in the unlisted tail
role_hints.note: Same structure as PT: licensed clinical time as the billed product. SLP has a meaningful teletherapy channel (schools, pediatrics) — delivery shifted online but still human-delivered.

role_hints.oews_basis: 31-2021 Physical Therapist Assistants 8.8% + 31-2011 Occupational Therapy Assistants 3.7%
role_hints.note: Licensed/certified hands-on extenders; Medicare already pays their modifier-flagged services at 85% — a reimbursement policy fact, not an AI effect.
Room setup, equipment turnover, scheduling support — partially absorbable by better scheduling and self-serve check-in.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-6013 Medical Secretaries 2.8% + 43-4171 Receptionists 1.6% + 43-9061 Office Clerks 1.4% + billing tail
role_hints.note: Scheduling, eligibility verification, prior authorization paperwork, claims and denials — the clean AI-automatable base; prior-auth automation is directly relevant given MA plans' heavy utilization management of therapy.
Compliance reporting and revenue-cycle oversight automate; referral-relationship management persists.

role_hints.oews_basis: unlisted tail (athletic trainers, exercise physiologists, techs, marketing)

source_hints.source: WebPT State of Rehab Therapy report (annual)
source_hints.why: The largest rehab-EMR vendor's practitioner survey — covers technology/AI adoption, documentation burden, burnout and clinic financial benchmarks.

source_hints.source: APTA (American Physical Therapy Association) workforce and private-practice section (APTA Private Practice) data
source_hints.why: Therapist demographics and private-practice owner data for succession evidence.

source_hints.source: CMS Medicare Physician Fee Schedule final rules (therapy code valuation, conversion factor updates)
source_hints.why: The primary document for the rate-setting mechanism — successive conversion-factor cuts and the PTA/OTA 85% differential are the payer-clawback channel in black and white.

source_hints.source: Provident Healthcare Partners outpatient rehabilitation M&A coverage
source_hints.why: Investment-bank tracking of PT/OT platform deals and multiple ranges by size tier.

source_hints.source: U.S. Physical Therapy, Inc. (NYSE: USPH) filings
source_hints.why: The public pure-play comp: real clinic-level economics, partnership acquisition model and disclosed purchase multiples for band-size targets.

source_hints.source: Digital MSK vendor outcome/utilization studies (Hinge Health, Sword Health public filings and peer-reviewed studies)
source_hints.why: The substitution channel's own evidence base — Hinge is public, so claimed visit-replacement rates can be checked against filings.

capture_questions: Rehab revenue is billed as licensed-therapist time in timed CPT units under the Medicare fee schedule (8-minute rule), commercial per-visit case rates, and workers'-comp fee schedules — since the billing unit is human time, AI cannot cut the billed input without cutting revenue; the capturable gain is reclaimed unbilled time (documentation) converted to more visits per therapist-day. Does the operator keep that throughput gain, or do payer-side mechanisms (annual conversion-factor cuts, MA utilization management, visit caps and prior auth) claw it back at the next rate cycle?

Who owns the patient flow: physician and surgeon referral relationships, health-system narrow networks, and direct-access consumer marketing — are these clinic-level assets that survive acquisition, or personal to individual referring relationships and therapists?

Digital MSK programs (Hinge, Sword) sold through employers and payers claim to replace a share of in-clinic visits with AI-guided home exercise — what does independent utilization evidence show about actual visit substitution vs additive use, and which visit types (low-acuity, chronic LBP) are exposed?

MA penetration and commercial plans push therapy into capped visit counts and prior-auth regimes — as AI makes clinics more efficient, do payers respond by tightening authorized visits (capturing the efficiency as reduced spend), and is there evidence of this already in MA therapy denial/authorization trends?
terminal_value_question: The product is licensed hands-on rehabilitation billed as therapist time in 15-minute CPT units — mature AI does not deliver manual therapy, and cutting therapist involvement cuts the billable unit itself, so the naive 'AI removes clinical labor' story is structurally wrong here. The mechanisms that actually decide terminal value: (a) payer rate-setting — CMS conversion-factor cuts and MA utilization management systematically transfer efficiency gains to payers; and (b) digital MSK substitution — AI-guided home exercise sold to employers/payers replacing a slice of low-acuity in-clinic visits. Is AI a throughput gain the operator keeps (documentation time reclaimed into billable visits), or a volume/rate squeeze the payers and platforms capture? Answer each mechanism separately with evidence.
special_notes: Billing-unit trap (spell out in pi_dist): time-based CPT billing means labor-substituting AI shrinks revenue, not cost — the honest V surface is the ~10-15% non-clinical wage share plus therapist throughput from reclaimed documentation time; do not apply an automatable fraction to the ~65% licensed-clinical wage share.

Reimbursement deflation predates AI: successive Medicare conversion-factor cuts and the PTA/OTA 85% payment differential compress margins independent of technology — don't attribute historical margin trends to AI adoption or its absence.

EBITDA normalization: owner-therapist clinical compensation sits inside small-clinic profit (SDE basis); normalize to market-rate therapist comp; USPH's disclosed partnership purchase multiples are a cleaner benchmark for band-size deals than broker-quoted SDE multiples.

Heterogeneity inside the code: outpatient ortho PT, pediatric speech (school contracts, Medicaid), OT hand therapy, and audiology (which sells hearing aids — a retail margin business, not a time business) differ sharply; the record's n_band 821 spans both the 100-499 (460 firms) and 500+ (361 firms) classes — identify the dominant sub-segment and set heterogeneous accordingly.

Boundary confusions: physician-owned PT (inside 621111), hospital outpatient therapy departments (622), SNF/home-health therapy (623/621610) all compete for the same referrals but sit outside this code; therapy-staffing agencies also blur survey data.
Consolidation (Upstream, ATI, PT Solutions, H2 Health, pediatric-therapy platforms; USPH's ongoing partnership roll-up) — B/M evidence only, never V/A (rule 2).
